params.description: Vertical picture graph appears on left. Favorite Fruits data. Scale of 2. Key visible: Each fruit symbol = 2 votes. Apples=3 symbols, Bananas=2 symbols, Oranges=2.5 symbols, Grapes=1.5 symbols.

Vertical picture graph appears on right. Same Favorite Fruits categories but different values. Scale of 5. Key visible and highlighted: Each fruit symbol = 5 votes. Apples=2 symbols, Bananas=1 symbol, Oranges=2 symbols, Grapes=1 symbol.

steps.text: In Warmup, you saw something important. Same data, but the scale-of-5 graph needed fewer symbols. That's because each symbol represents MORE.
Same data, fewer symbols. That's why scale of 5 is useful when numbers get bigger. What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?
What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?
Each symbol represents 5 items. That's the scale.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Favorite Fruits with scale of 2. Key visible.
Vertical picture graph showing Favorite Fruits with scale of 5. Key highlighted.
Vertical picture graph on left showing Favorite Fruits data with scale of 2. Multiple choice prompt active.
Vertical picture graph on right showing Favorite Fruits data with scale of 5. Key highlighted. Multiple choice prompt active.

params.description: Data table appears. Favorite Playground Activities. Swings: 20, Slides: 15, Monkey Bars: 25, Sandbox: 10. Vertical layout.
Empty vertical picture graph appears. Favorite Playground Activities. Key visible: Each star symbol = 5. All columns empty, ready for grouping animation.

steps.text: Let's see how 20 items become symbols when the scale is 5. Watch — we'll group by 5s.

params.description: 20 individual items appear for Swings category.
Items group into 4 groups of 5 each. Guide counts aloud during animation.

steps.text: 5, 10, 15, 20. That's 4 groups of 5.

params.description: 4 groups transform into 4 star symbols stacking vertically in Swings column.

steps.text: 20 items equals 4 symbols. Each symbol shows 5.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values.
Vertical picture graph with Swings column showing 4 symbols. Other columns empty. Key visible: Each star = 5.

steps.text: It is easier to read this picture graph if you can count by 5s. That's exactly what we do with scale of 5.
Let's check: does the picture graph match our data table? 4 symbols, scale of 5. Count by 5s with me. 4 symbols, counting by 5s. What's the total?
What is the total represented by the picture graph?
20 items. 5, 10, 15, 20. Skip-counting tells you the total in the picture graph and that matches the value in the data table.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values.
Vertical picture graph. Swings column with 4 symbols confirmed as 20. Other columns empty.
Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values. Multiple choice prompt active.
Vertical picture graph. Swings column showing 4 symbols. Multiple choice prompt active.

steps.text: Slides has 15. How many symbols is that?
Think: count by 5s until you reach 15. How many 5s? Click to set the height of the Slides.
Set the number of symbols for Slides.
3 symbols. 5, 10, 15. You used skip-counting to figure it out.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values.
Vertical picture graph. Swings column: 4 symbols. Slides column: 3 symbols (student-created). Monkey Bars and Sandbox empty.
Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values. Click-to-set-height tool active on Slides.
Vertical picture graph. Swings: 4 symbols. Slides column active for student input. Monkey Bars and Sandbox empty.

steps.text: Monkey Bars has 25. That's a bigger number. Click to set the height of the Monkey Bars.
Set the height for Monkey Bars.
5 symbols for 25. You counted: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values.
Vertical picture graph. Swings: 4 symbols. Slides: 3 symbols. Monkey Bars: 5 symbols (student-created). Sandbox empty.
Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values. Click-to-set-height tool active on Monkey Bars.
Vertical picture graph. Swings: 4 symbols. Slides: 3 symbols. Monkey Bars column active for student input. Sandbox empty.

steps.text: Let's complete the picture graph. Sandbox has 10. That's the smallest number. Click to set the height for Sandbox.
Set the height for Sandbox.
2 symbols for 10. Count by 5s: 5, 10.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values.
Completed vertical picture graph. Swings: 4 symbols. Slides: 3 symbols. Monkey Bars: 5 symbols. Sandbox: 2 symbols.
Data table showing Favorite Playground Activities values. Click-to-set-height tool active on Sandbox.
Vertical picture graph. Swings: 4 symbols. Slides: 3 symbols. Monkey Bars: 5 symbols. Sandbox column active for student input.

steps.text: You made a scaled picture graph. Let's read it. Which activity is the most popular?
Click on the activity that is most popular.

validator.description: Student clicked Monkey Bars — correct, 5 symbols = 25

steps.text: Monkey Bars has 5 symbols, that's 25. The tallest column has the most votes.

elements.description: Completed vertical picture graph. Monkey Bars column highlighted as most popular.
Completed vertical picture graph. Swings: 4 symbols. Slides: 3 symbols. Monkey Bars: 5 symbols. Sandbox: 2 symbols. Key visible. Click_category tool active.

steps.text: And which got the fewest?
Click on the category with the fewest.
Sandbox has only 2 symbols, that's 10 votes.

elements.description: Completed vertical picture graph. Sandbox column highlighted as having fewest votes.
Completed vertical picture graph. All columns visible. Click_category tool active for fewest question.

steps.text: You made a picture graph with scale of 5. Now let's see ANOTHER way to show the exact same data.

elements.description: Completed vertical picture graph from Section 1. Favorite Playground Activities data.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears on right side. Same Playground Activities data. Axis labeled 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. All 4 bars pre-filled: Swings=20, Slides=15, Monkey Bars=25, Sandbox=10.
Data table appears alongside both graphs showing same Playground Activities values.

steps.text: You've read bar graphs before. Here's the same Playground data as a bar graph.
In a picture graph, you count by 5s using SYMBOLS: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.

In a bar graph, you count by 5s using the numbers written along the side of the graph on the AXIS. Look — 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. Same skip-counting, just written on the side. Both graphs count by 5s. Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?
Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?
The axis shows the skip-counting: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. That's your scale of 5 — built right into the side of the graph.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph on left. Completed Playground Activities data.
Vertical bar graph on right. Axis highlighted. All bars pre-filled.
Data table showing Playground Activities values.
Vertical picture graph on left. Completed Playground Activities data. Multiple choice prompt active.
Vertical bar graph on right. All bars pre-filled. Multiple choice prompt active.
Data table showing Playground Activities values alongside both graphs.

steps.text: In a bar graph, you read the HEIGHT, where the bar ENDS on the axis. Look at Slides. What's the HEIGHT of the Slides bar? Where does it end?
What's the HEIGHT of the Slides bar?
15. The bar's HEIGHT is 15. It ends at 15 on the axis. Same as 3 symbols on the picture graph. Both show 15.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph. Slides category highlighted showing 3 symbols.
Vertical bar graph. Slides bar highlighted, height at 15.
Vertical picture graph. Slides category highlighted. Multiple choice prompt active.
Vertical bar graph. Slides bar highlighted. Multiple choice prompt active.

params.description: Data table appears. Favorite Lunch Foods. Pizza: 20, Tacos: 30, Salad: 15, Pasta: 25, Sandwiches: 15. Vertical layout.
Vertical bar graph appears. Axis labeled 0 to 35 in 5s. Axis numbers highlighted. Tacos bar pre-filled at 30. Sandwiches bar pre-filled at 15. Pizza, Salad, Pasta bars empty.

steps.text: Before you create the bars, look at the numbers on the axis.
These numbers count by 5s — just like you did with symbols. The axis does the skip-counting for you. What comes after 20 on this axis?
What comes after 20 on this axis?
25. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. Same skip-counting you used for symbols, just written on the side.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Lunch Foods values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis highlighted. Tacos: 30, Sandwiches: 15 pre-filled.
Data table showing Favorite Lunch Foods values. Multiple choice prompt active.
Vertical bar graph. Axis highlighted. Tacos and Sandwiches pre-filled. Multiple choice prompt active.

steps.text: Pizza has 20 votes. You found 20 on the axis. Set the height for Pizza to show 20.
Set the height for Pizza to show 20.

validator.description: Student set bar at height 20 — correct

steps.text: The bar ends at 20 — right on the axis. The skip-counting is already there; you just match the height.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Lunch Foods values.
Vertical bar graph. Pizza bar now at 20. Tacos: 30, Sandwiches: 15 pre-filled. Salad and Pasta empty.
Data table showing Favorite Lunch Foods values. Click-to-set-height tool active on Pizza.
Vertical bar graph. Pizza bar active for student input. Tacos and Sandwiches pre-filled.

steps.text: Finish the graph. Salad has 15, Pasta has 25. Set the heights for Salad and Pasta.
Set the heights for Salad and Pasta.

validator.description: Student set both bars correctly — Salad at 15, Pasta at 25

steps.text: You built the bar graph. Each bar shows exactly how many votes that food got.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Lunch Foods values.
Completed vertical bar graph. Pizza: 20, Tacos: 30, Salad: 15, Pasta: 25, Sandwiches: 15.
Data table showing Favorite Lunch Foods values. Click-to-set-height tool active on Salad and Pasta.
Vertical bar graph. Pizza: 20. Tacos: 30. Sandwiches: 15. Salad and Pasta bars active for student input.

params.description: Vertical picture graph appears alongside bar graph. Same Favorite Lunch Foods data. Scale of 5. Key visible: Each star = 5. Pizza: 4 symbols, Tacos: 6 symbols, Salad: 3 symbols, Pasta: 5 symbols, Sandwiches: 3 symbols.

steps.text: Here's the same data as a picture graph. Let's check: do they match? Look at Pasta. The bar shows 25. How many symbols should the picture graph have?
How many symbols should the picture graph have?
5 symbols. Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. There are 5 fives. Bar graph and picture graph: same information, different format.

elements.description: Data table showing Favorite Lunch Foods values.
Completed vertical bar graph. Pasta bar at 25.
Vertical picture graph. Pasta column showing 5 symbols confirmed.
Data table showing Favorite Lunch Foods values. Multiple choice prompt active.
Completed vertical bar graph. Multiple choice prompt active.
Vertical picture graph. Same data displayed alongside bar graph.

steps.text: You already know how to compare using graphs. Same skill works here. How many MORE students chose Tacos than Salad?
How many MORE students chose Tacos than Salad?
15 more. Tacos has 30, Salad has 15. The difference is 15.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Tacos and Salad bars highlighted.
Vertical bar graph. Tacos and Salad bars highlighted. Multiple choice prompt active.

steps.text: So far, some parts of the graphs were already filled in for you. Now let's see if you can build a whole graph on your own.

params.description: Data table appears. Books Read This Month. Liam: 35, Emma: 20, Noah: 25, Olivia: 40. Vertical layout.
Horizontal picture graph appears. All rows empty. Key visible: Each star = 5 books.

steps.text: This graph goes sideways — horizontal. Same idea, you just click for the length for the number of symbols instead of height.
Make a picture graph. Start with Liam. He read 35 books. Set the number of symbols for Liam.
Set the number of symbols for Liam.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Horizontal picture graph. Liam row: 7 symbols. Emma, Noah, Olivia rows empty.
Data table showing Books Read This Month values. Click-to-set-length tool active on Liam.
Horizontal picture graph. Liam row active for student input. Other rows empty.

steps.text: Finish the picture graph. Use the data table and complete the graph.
Set the lengths for all remaining categories.

validator.description: Student set all rows correctly — Emma: 4, Noah: 5, Olivia: 8

steps.text: You built the whole graph. Each symbol shows 5, and you used skip-counting to figure out how many.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Completed horizontal picture graph. Liam: 7 symbols, Emma: 4 symbols, Noah: 5 symbols, Olivia: 8 symbols.
Data table showing Books Read This Month values. Click-to-set-length tool active on Emma, Noah, Olivia.
Horizontal picture graph. Liam: 7 symbols. Emma, Noah, Olivia rows active for student input.

steps.text: Use your graph to find how many FEWER books Emma read than Olivia.
How many FEWER books did Emma read compared to Olivia?
20 fewer. Olivia read 40. Emma read 20. The difference is 20.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph. Emma and Olivia rows highlighted.
Completed horizontal picture graph. Emma and Olivia rows highlighted. Multiple choice prompt active.

params.description: Data table reappears showing Books Read This Month values.
Scale toggle control appears with three options: Each star = 1, Each star = 2, Each star = 5. Currently set to scale of 5.

steps.text: You built this graph with scale of 5. But what would it look like with a different scale? Try it.
Toggle the scale to 1. What happens?

params.description: Graph animates: symbols multiply. Liam: 7 to 35, Emma: 4 to 20, Noah: 5 to 25, Olivia: 8 to 40. Key updates to Each star = 1.

steps.text: Olivia went from 8 symbols to 40! That's a LOT of symbols. Now try scale of 2.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Horizontal picture graph now at scale of 1. Liam: 35 symbols, Emma: 20 symbols, Noah: 25 symbols, Olivia: 40 symbols.
Scale toggle now showing scale of 1 selected.
Data table showing Books Read This Month values. Scale toggle prompt active.
Horizontal picture graph at scale of 5. Scale toggle prompt active.
Scale toggle control showing scale of 5. Student can toggle to 1 or 2.

steps.text: Now toggle to scale of 2.

params.description: Graph animates: symbols reduce. Liam: 35 to 17.5, Emma: 20 to 10, Noah: 25 to 12.5, Olivia: 40 to 20. Half-symbols appear for odd-divided values. Key updates to Each star = 2.

steps.text: Fewer symbols — but still more than scale of 5. Toggle back to 5.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Horizontal picture graph now at scale of 2. Liam: 17.5 symbols, Emma: 10 symbols, Noah: 12.5 symbols, Olivia: 20 symbols.
Scale toggle now showing scale of 2 selected.
Data table showing Books Read This Month values. Scale toggle prompt active.
Horizontal picture graph at scale of 1. Scale toggle prompt active.
Scale toggle control showing scale of 1. Student can toggle to 2 or 5.

steps.text: Toggle back to scale of 5.

validator.description: Student toggles back to scale of 5

params.description: Graph animates back to original state. Liam: 17.5 to 7, Emma: 10 to 4, Noah: 12.5 to 5, Olivia: 20 to 8. Key updates to Each star = 5.

steps.text: Same 40 books for Olivia every time. Scale of 1: 40 symbols. Scale of 2: 20 symbols. Scale of 5: just 8.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Horizontal picture graph back at scale of 5. Liam: 7 symbols, Emma: 4 symbols, Noah: 5 symbols, Olivia: 8 symbols.
Scale toggle now showing scale of 5 selected.
Data table showing Books Read This Month values. Scale toggle prompt active.
Horizontal picture graph at scale of 2. Scale toggle prompt active.
Scale toggle control showing scale of 2. Student can toggle to 1 or 5.

steps.text: Which scale uses the fewest symbols?
Which scale uses the fewest symbols?
Scale of 5. Bigger scale, fewer symbols — same data.
All three showed the same information. The scale just changes how many symbols you need. Now let's use YOUR graph to answer some questions.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Horizontal picture graph at scale of 5. Scale toggle removed.
Data table showing Books Read This Month values. Multiple choice prompt active.
Horizontal picture graph at scale of 5. Multiple choice prompt active.
Scale toggle control showing scale of 5 selected.

params.description: Data table appears. Weekend Screen Time (minutes). Saturday: 35, Sunday: 30, Monday: 40, Tuesday: 25. Horizontal layout.
Horizontal bar graph appears. Axis along bottom labeled 0 to 45 in 5s. All 4 bars empty.

steps.text: This bar graph is horizontal — the bars go sideways. But look at the axis along the bottom. Same skip-counting by 5s. What comes after 35 on this axis?
What comes after 35 on this axis?
40. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15... 35, 40, 45. Same pattern, just running sideways.

elements.description: Data table showing Weekend Screen Time values.
Horizontal bar graph. Axis highlighted. All bars empty.
Data table showing Weekend Screen Time values. Multiple choice prompt active.
Horizontal bar graph. Axis highlighted. All bars empty. Multiple choice prompt active.

steps.text: Now a bar graph, also horizontal. Read the data, set the lengths of the bars to complete the graph.
Create the bar graph. Set all four bars.
You created the bar graph independently. Horizontal or vertical — you've got this.

elements.description: Data table showing Weekend Screen Time values.
Completed horizontal bar graph. Saturday: 35, Sunday: 30, Monday: 40, Tuesday: 25.
Data table showing Weekend Screen Time values. Click-to-set-length tool active on all bars.
Horizontal bar graph. All bars active for student input.

steps.text: How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday IN ALL?
How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday IN ALL?
65 minutes in all. Saturday has 35, Sunday has 30. 35 plus 30 is 65.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph. Saturday and Sunday bars highlighted.
Completed horizontal bar graph. Saturday and Sunday bars highlighted. Multiple choice prompt active.

params.description: Horizontal picture graph from earlier (Books Read) appears alongside bar graph.

steps.text: You made both kinds of graphs today. Here's one last question. Picture graphs use symbols. Bar graphs use bars. What do these graphs have in common?
What do these graphs have in common?

tool.name: multiple_choice
options: They both use the same scale

condition.selected: They both use the same scale

validator.description: Student selected 'They both use the same scale' — correct

steps.text: Both use scale of 5. Picture graphs show it with symbols — count by 5s. Bar graphs show it on the side. Same scale, different format.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Weekend Screen Time.
Horizontal picture graph showing Books Read This Month.
Horizontal bar graph showing Weekend Screen Time. Multiple choice prompt active.
Horizontal picture graph showing Books Read This Month. Multiple choice prompt active.

steps.text: You made picture graphs and bar graphs, both with scale of 5. You used skip-counting to figure out symbols and bar heights. Let's see what you know.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Weekend Screen Time.
Horizontal picture graph showing Books Read This Month.
